title: PH Unveils National Cybersecurity Plan 2023–2028
body: Written by: Euri Barbon Edited by: Abram Marcelo DICT building at Quezon City from Jun Sanchez On April 4, 2024, the Philippines launched the National Cybersecurity Plan (NCSP) 2023–2028 under the Executive Order #58 to enhance the nation’s cybersecurity landscape. Developed by the Department of Information and Communications Technology (DICT), the NCSP focuses on establishing a secure and resilient digital ecosystem for all Filipinos. The DICT aims to boost the cybersecurity workforce through capacity-building programs, establishing a Cybersecurity Center of Excellence, and implementing a six-stage incident response model — Identify, Contain, Analyze, Eradicate, Recover, and Lesson Learned. Key components of the NCSP include establishing a National Cybersecurity Intelligence Fusion Center and a network of Computer Emergency Response Teams. These initiatives shall enable better monitoring, response, and mitigation of cyber incidents across various sectors. In addition, October will be recognized as Cybersecurity Awareness Month to promote public awareness on online safety. Industry leaders, including cybersecurity experts from Kaspersky, support the NCSP, emphasizing its role in safeguarding national infrastructure against evolving cybercrimes. As Genie Sugene Gan, Head of Government Affairs and Public Policy of Kaspersky, stated, 'The NCSP provides a strategic framework for addressing cyber threats comprehensively and ensuring the resilience of our digital infrastructure, and we are optimistic about its effective implementation.' Last 2023, the Philippines experienced the highest number of cyberattacks in Southeast Asia, with 68% of companies experiencing at least one cybersecurity incident including malware, phishing attacks, and password breaches. Senator Win Gatchalian recently expressed concern on the country’s urgency on cybersecurity. 'There should be a sense of urgency because the more that the world is connected, the more that we are prone to cyberattacks. We should be wary of this type of vulnerability in our country', he said. Previously, Sen. Gatchalian advocated for the Critical Information Infrastructure Act of 2022 which enforced the adoption of minimum information security standards, responding to cybersecurity incidents, and designation of skilled personnel with cybersecurity credentials PH Unveils National Cybersecurity Plan 2023–2028 by Euri Barbon DOST broaches ‘ISIP Program,’ ‘Project Marayum’ for natural language research by Abram Josh Marcelo

title: DOST broaches ‘ISIP Program,’ ‘Project Marayum’ for natural language research
body: Written by: Abram Josh Marcelo Edited by: Elijah Mejilla DOST and AI-related institutions discuss Large Language Models for Philippine Languages. Photo fom DOST-PCIEERD. In August 2024, the DOST Philippine Council for Industry, Energy, and Emerging Technology Research and Development (PCIEERD) held a multi-disciplinary meeting tackling various projects to lay out a roadmap for natural language processing (NLP) and large language model development focused on Philippine languages. The forum highlighted the Interdisciplinary Signal Processing for Pinoys: Software Applications for Education (ISIP: SAFE) Program, which developed reading and writing tutors for children and captioning systems for Philippine languages. Researchers from University of the Philippines Diliman (UPD) — Electrical and Electronics Engineering Institute and De La Salle University — College of Computer Studies (DLSU-CCS) implemented ISIP: SAFE. Speakers further raised attention to Project Marayum — a community-built and curated online dictionary for languages in the country — developed by UPD graduates. FilWordNet by DLSU-CCS also gained recognition, it is a language resource that tracks word sense changes on the internet using network science and NLP. DOST PCIEERD Executive Director Enrico C. Paringit emphasized the significance of these efforts to the country’s cultural heritage. 'This is an opportunity for us to maximize the use of technologies available to us in preserving and propagating our different languages. Through this roadmap, we can identify research gaps and possible solutions in terms of technology, human resource, and policy,' said Paringit. The meeting also mentioned the Mindanao Natural Language Processing Research and Development Laboratory funded by DOST. The said laboratory aims to preserve endangered languages from Mindanao using NLP. Projects collaboratively built by researchers from other universities and industry cohorts took a share of the forum’s time as well. 'We thank our researchers and partners from the academe, government, and industry in making solid efforts in providing solutions and opening opportunities for the Filipino people to utilize science, technology, and innovation in this initiative,' DOST Secretary Renato U. Solidum, Jr added. Representatives from the Komisyon sa Wikang Filipino, AI Singapore, and academic institutions such as the Ateneo Social Computing Science Laboratory and Ateneo Center for Computing Competency and Research attended said event, endorsing the planned research roadmap. PH Unveils National Cybersecurity Plan 2023–2028 by Euri Barbon DOST broaches ‘ISIP Program,’ ‘Project Marayum’ for natural language research by Abram Josh Marcelo

title: Bootcamp 11.0: DCS welcomes Batch 2024
body: Written by: Eriene Galinato, and Gabriel Inigo De Guzman Edited by: Abram Marcelo Graphics by Stephen Gonda Last August 7–9, the incoming Department of Computer Science held Bootcamp 11.0: the annual three-day introductory event for freshies, shiftees, and transferees (FSTs). Bootcamp’s 11th year took place at the UP Alumni Engineers Centennial Hall (UP AECH), and takes inspiration from Alice in Wonderland. The event opened with a short speech from this year’s Overall Head, Drei Ragasa. For the first day, FSTs formed 9 teams, named after various characters in Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland and Through the Looking Glass. As their first team-building event, the FSTs created and then presented their respective team cheers. Alumnus Basti Ortiz then guided FSTs down the rabbit hole of UP Diliman’s BS Computer Science program. Ortiz highlighted how the curriculum focuses on both theory and research, requiring students to apply their knowledge and understand software complexity. He also addressed some common misconceptions and finally clarified the goals of DCS. The day continued with speakers Miko Surara and Daryll Ko serving some basic Python and Object-Oriented Programming to the FSTs. Relating their topics to the theme: just as one would need weapons to slay a Jabberwock — one would need some basic knowledge of programming to slay their early academic trials in BS Computer Science. To end the day on a high note, the teams battled it out in a prompt engineering competition hosted by the UP Association of Computer Science Majors (UP CURSOR). In ten alternating rounds, each team had to ideate a prompt in one minute to try to crack the passwords hidden by other teams during attack rounds or hide their own password during defense rounds. Bootcamp’s second day kicked off with a strong sense of camaraderie and humor as the FSTs participated in a Wackathon organized by the UP Center for Student Innovations (UP CSI). In UP CSI’s Wackathon, each group proposed an app with the theme of ending the human race , down the rabbit hole of chaos. Following the Wackathon, alumna speaker Shannen Tan brought the students back to the real (virtual) world in her talk about the many different rabbit holes and career paths that BS CS graduates have landed in. Organizers then toured the FSTs around UP AECH, with each stop featuring a description of activities that take place in areas visited. The tour concluded in the Accenture Ideas Exchange Room, which was followed by team-building activities such as teacup presentation. Each group had a day to decorate their teacups, with the design relating to the theme of the Bootcamp 11.0 (Wonderland). The finished cups served as flags to represent their respective teams. Keeping up camaraderie while drawing Day 2 to a close, the UP Association of Computing Machinery (UP ACM) hosted an interactive game where teams had to generate a very specific word, phrase, or response from ChatGPT. To add an extra layer of challenge, certain words were banned, making it more intriguing to win the game. On Bootcamp 11.0’s final day, Tea Time Trials challenged the nine groups of FSTs with an Amazing Race-like competition filled with various tasks around the department. Special challenges surprised the players, offering opportunities for teams to get ahead of their opponents. These challenges lie in the Frabjous Cheer, where each team quickly created a unique cheer based on their name, and Party Eleganze, where teams designed a coordinated costume around a chosen theme to wear. In culmination to the event, Batch 2021 representative Paolo De Los Santos led the talk ‘Through the Looking Glass,’ during which he highlighted the importance of self-care and time blocking, sharing to the FSTs tips on surviving their first year in UP Diliman. Bootcamp 11.0 wrapped up with an awarding ceremony, where each FST group received certificates for their warm three-day participation. Although the event has ended, it marks the beginning of an exciting journey of Batch 2024 at the Department of Computer Science — a true wonderland of opportunities. Bootcamp 11.0: DCS welcomes Batch 2024 by Eriene Galinato, and Gabriel Inigo De Guzman

title: Turing Women: Triumphs in Mending the Gap
body: As we head towards the end of Women’s Month, we highlight the impact and significance that women hold in the field computer science. In a scientific field rife with gender inequality and discrimination, these women have established their legacies through their intellect and passion for computing. Their pioneering works have paved the way for major developments in cryptography, compiler optimization, and distributed systems. Join us in celebrating the three women who have been awarded the highest distinction in computer science—the Turing Award. Written by: Aneko Delfin Edited by: Abram Marcelo Graphics by John Marck Palpal-latoc A million-dollar sponsorship from Google, a trophy from the world’s largest computing society, and a place among the most influential figures in the field: these are what the Turing Award offers its laureates, often regarded as the Nobel Prize in Computing . Yet, out of more than 70 recipients, only three have been women. In fact, it has been over a decade since the last female computer scientist received this honor. A telltale sign, a blaring hint to the systemic bias against women that has long aggravated the gender gap in computing. This imbalance is exactly why Francis Allen, Barbara Liskov, and Shafi Goldwasser deserve greater recognition, not only for their groundbreaking contributions but also for marking their legacy as women who transformed the field of computing. Frances Allen Image source: https://newsarchive.berkeley.edu/news/berkeleyan/2008/01/23_allen.shtml In 2006, Frances Allen became the first woman to receive the Turing Award, recognized for her work in compiler optimization. Her research revolutionized the way programs are translated into machine code, making software run more efficiently on modern hardware. Allen started her professional journey as a mathematics teacher. To pay off her student debt, however, she shifted careers by joining IBM as a researcher. She intended to return to teaching, but her experience at IBM redefined her goals and passion, which led to her becoming a leading figure in compiler optimization, with the company dubbing her as an ‘accidental’ scientist. Her work laid the groundwork for the compilers we use today, which are essential for translating code from high-level programming languages into machine-executable instructions. Allen was also an avid adventurer. She traveled internationally not only to give lectures about compilers, but also to pursue her passion for mountain climbing. 'I love to climb mountains and explore different parts of the world because I like exploring things I don’t know or understand,' she shared during an NYU interview . Throughout her career, Allen also championed efforts to support women and minorities in technology. She mentored countless young researchers and was an active advocate for diversity and inclusion in computer science. In addition to her personal mentorship, Allen played a key role in shaping technology policy through her work on the National Research Council’s Computer Science and Telecommunications Board. Her contributions to fostering diversity were recognized through the establishment of the Frances E. Allen IBM Women in Technology Mentoring Award, which honors individuals who demonstrate exceptional commitment to mentoring women in technology. Her legacy extends beyond her technical innovations, inspiring future generations of women to pursue careers in the field. Barbara Liskov Image source: https://news.mit.edu/2009/turing-liskov-0310 Barbara Liskov became the second woman to receive the Turing Award in 2008, recognized for her pioneering work in programming languages and system design. Her research laid the foundations for object-oriented programming and software development practices. Liskov’s career in computing began at a time when few women entered the field. In the 1960s, she applied to graduate mathematics programs at Princeton University but was ultimately rejected because the institution did not admit female students in mathematics at the time. Despite this, she applied to Stanford University where she became one of the first women in the US to earn a PhD in computer science. She later joined the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), where her research would change the way programmers design and structure software systems. One of her most influential contributions is the development of the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP), a key concept in maintaining and modifying code without breaking the system. She also invented the concept of data abstraction, introduced through her work on the CLU programming language; It laid the groundwork for modular programming and has influenced modern languages like Java, C++, and Python. Beyond her technical achievements, Liskov has also dedicated much of her career to mentoring and advocating for greater gender equity in computer science, having served as Associate Provost for Faculty Equity at MIT. Her work has not only transformed the field but also opened doors for future generations of women in technology. Shafi Goldwasser Image source: https://www.technologyreview.com/2019/08/21/238665/shafi-goldwasser/ Shafi Goldwasser joined the list in 2012, receiving the award for her contributions to cryptography and computational number theory. Her research, which established the theoretical foundations for modern cryptography, paved the way for computer scientists to explore methods for securing code and digital information. What sparked Goldwasser’s curiosity in computer science was an algorithms and discrete math course she took while pursuing a degree in mathematics. Her growing interest in the field led her to enroll in graduate school for computer science at the University of California, Berkeley. From there, Goldwasser became a faculty member at MIT, immersing herself in cryptograph theories such as encryption systems (e.g., RSA), quadratic residuosity, and interactive proofs. She helped develop tools that could verify data without disclosure between network entities and provided the foundation for solving hard problems like the primality test. Even before winning the Turing Award, Goldwasser had already won the Gödel Prize—twice. As a woman in a male-dominated field, Goldwasser recalls asserting to her colleagues that she was 'someone to be listened to and reckoned with.' She further stressed the weight of addressing such prejudice, stating, 'We must keep raising this disparity throughout our scientific community to emphasize the need for change, supported by evidence. Achieving gender equality in science will lead to better science, a better workforce, and better educated children in future generations.' Painfully Few, But Huge Steps Allen, raised a farmer’s daughter; Liskov, who faced gender bias firsthand; and Goldwasser, entering with no prior programming experience—all reached the podium and defined the foundations that computer science students now take as gospel. They have even managed to create entire subfields in a field where stereotypes continue to diminish women’s scientific talent. These major wins hold more than just symbolic value; they are not just rare moments to be temporarily celebrated, virtue-signaled and forgotten. They impart a roadmap for progress to society, one that we hope will elevate the contributions of women in STEM. The fact that women have comprised just 4% of winners over half a century remains disappointing. Still, these pioneers have undeniably inspired generations of women to come. It’s long overdue for us to amplify the ongoing call to nominate more women and demand equal representation in both academia and industry. Just as these transformative trio devised proofs to solve problems in computer science, let their footprint prove that the hurdles to inclusivity can and must be broken. Turing Women: Triumphs in Mending the Gap by Aneko Delfin

title: Tempus : Computer Science Week 2017
body: Last 24th of April, the Department of Computer Science celebrated the annual Computer Science Week. This year’s theme – Tempus, revolved around the four seasons of the year to represent each batch. In cooperation with the Computer Science Network, each day had unique events hosted by a sponsoring organization. The UP Center for Student Innovations held the week-long DCS Career Fair from April 24 to 28. The fair aimed to have the CS students, especially juniors and seniors, prepared for internship and employment by having company talks, exams and mock interviews. Voting for the CS Pop Icon, the annual department awards for all members of the department, started on April 25, and ran for three days. On the same day, the UP Association of Computer Science Majors also hosted the CS Batch Wars: a twenty-four hour friendly inter-batch competition. Each batch is tasked to take selfies and videos of themselves doing challenges and posting to Facebook to garner points. Receiving a total score of 725 points, the 2013 batch nabbed the first place. On the night of April 27, Eclipse: The CS Week Party took place at Merci Restaurant and Bar in Timog Avenue. Organized by the Computer Science Servers, this party was free from admission fees and open to all CS students. Twenty-one nominees for the CS Pop Icon were also awarded, with overwhelming votes reaching more than a thousand in total. Students, faculty members and organizers all gathered together playing games, enjoying the music and performances and merrymaking the entire night. On April 29, the UP Association for Computer Machinery hosted Haxxor 2.0: a computer security and ethical hacking workshop for students, Capture-the-flag hacking competition. Participants for the competition are tasked to showcase their learnings from the morning seminar by hacking a website and capturing flags to gain points. After three hours of intense competition, one team of third years won the top prize among fifteen teams by capturing a total of nine flags. Concluding the event for the Computer Science Week was a cyber-security seminar hosted by TrendMicro, a sponsor for the CS Week. The entirety of the Computer Science Week was event-filled and action-packed – a variety of workshops and competitions with a grand party – the best way to celebrate for every member in the department. Tempus: The UP Diliman Computer Science Week 2017 was presented by the UP Department of Computer Science Servers and co-presented by The UP Parser, UP Association for Computer Machinery, UP Association of Computer Science Majors, UP Center for Student Innovations, TrendMicro and Figaro Coffee. Article by: Mark Daniel Asiddao

title: GeeCS 9.0

title: Sweet Dreams: A Night of Fun for Graduates
body: A cozy, laid-back setting welcomed the graduating students of the Department of Computer Science at 3B Rest House, Antipolo City. Last June 23-24, the Computer Science Representatives along with the Department of Computer Science Servers (DCSS) prepared a night for them to chill before they go on to a new adventure as professionals. With its theme 'Sweet Dreams', the event aims to give the students a night where they can peacefully sleep away from workloads bothering their minds. Dinner was served as soon as the students arrived. Afterwards, they proceed to the videoke to take a break while the volunteers are preparing for the program. Some professors also came to join the fun. Formally starting the program, Carlo La Rosa, one of the CS Reps, gave his opening remarks. Then it was followed by Sir Mario Carreon’s highly anticipated speech in every Grad. Dinner. The program continued with having the graduates play 'Wowowee' games. They enjoyed playing 'Hep, Hep, Hooray', 'Pera o’ Bayong', and other games; as well as listening to the show’s signature songs such as 'Beep, Beep, Beep, Ang Sabi Ng Jeep', 'Boom Tarat Tarat', etc. Afterwards, Ma’am Rae Pineda shared her message privately to the graduates while chilling beside the pool. 'Pinakahighlight for me wasn’t the program itself though fun and kita naman yung effort nung volunteers. Ang naaalala ko sa gabing yon ay yung after program kung saan nagkekwentuhan na lang yung mga grad. Nakakatuwa na meron akong mga nakausap na kahit 4 years kaming batchmates at naging classmates ko pa yung iba, doon ko lang talaga nakakwentuhan,' Micah Bunag, one of the graduates, stated. As she said, the rest of the night was spent on bonding among graduates with having them get along with their other batchmates that they don’t get to know better in their stay in UP. This event serves not only it’s goal to provide them comfort and relaxation, but also made a way for them to interact with their other batchmates. Article by: Rogiella Isidro

title: ESC brings Heyday: Eng’g Opening Week 2016
body: To uphold camaraderie and establish rapport among the whole Engineering community as we welcome a new academic year, the UP Engineering Student Council (ESC) holds its annual week-long event entitled 'Heyday: Engineering Opening Week 2016' last September 5-9, 2016. Students from the Department of Computer Science (DCS) took part in the grand celebration of the biggest college in the university, by participating in this year’s lineup of events with the theme county fair – the Sportsfest, Engineering Academic Fair, Kickoff: Opening Exhibit, Heads Up!: Service Booths, Leg’go: Engineering Library Tour, Hop In: Organization Fair, and Chill-out: Culminating Night. After a series of intense games and heated matches in different sports fields like basketball, volleyball, football, ultimate, e-sports, and even mini games, the Electrical and Electronic Engineering Institute (EEEI) emerged as the overall champions for the Sportsfest, with the Institute of Civil Engineering (ICE) following close as 1 st runner-up and the Department of Mining, Metallurgical, and Materials Engineering (DMMME) and the Department of Mechanical Engineering (DME) tying as 2 nd runners-up. Aside from the college intramurals, ESC organized its first ever Engineering Academic Fair, wherein scholarships and other opportunities are opened to the students. The 155 Engineering freshmen were also catered to in 'Kickoff', as they engaged in the opening exhibit and college orientation in order to know further about their chosen degree programs. Embodying a spirit of service, ESC also launched various booths of government and non-government agencies such as NBI, SSS, and DFA to accommodate the inquiries of students and faculty alike. Furthermore, the Leg’go: Engineering Library Tour was also conducted in order to present the facilities of the college’s libraries and their wide array of references and services offered to engineering students. The Organization Fair, on the other hand, showcased 30 of the many engineering organizations which aim to nurture the students’ growth and character development, including the UP Association for Computing Machinery (UP ACM) and the UP Association of Computer Science Majors (UP CURSOR). Ending the celebration with a bang, performers from different organizations partook in the Culminating Night held at the CS Amphitheater, wherein they exhibited their talents in the musical aspect as the whole engineering crowd jammed to their songs. All photos courtesy of the Engineering Student Council.

title: Doors Unlocked: Eng’g OWeek Kicks Off
body: To welcome the enthusiastic freshmen from the College of Engineering, the Engineering Student Council (ESC) held its annual Engineering Opening Week from August 29 to September 5. Dubbed with the theme 'Alohomora,' the week-long event provided an avenue for the Engineering freshies to boost their bloc’s collective effort and to further cultivate their bonds as a team. Braced with outstanding team chemistry, Bloc G29, which was handled by Engage UP, emerged as the Best Bloc in the Department of Computer Science as well as the Best Horcrux Hunters. In addition, the CS Department also triumphed in the Quidditch Cup as the 3 rd runner-up. The OWeek consisted of seven events that the college’s freshies eagerly took part in. The Platform 9 ¾ served as the opener wherein several organizations in the College of Engineering namely Inhenyero, Engineering Registration Committee (RegCom), and UP CAPES each disclosed their programs and major events. In addition, the candidates vying for the position of Engineering Freshie Rep were also introduced. An expo of service-oriented booths, Diagon Alley, was also one of the highlights of this celebration. Booths like the ‘#BeBotante’ voter’s registration, the ‘Sanduguan’ blood drive and ‘Paglingap: Magpa-Eye-os’ eye check-up with the support of the UP Red Cross Youth were open to the whole UP Community throughout the week. Furthermore, prominent organizations in the college, like the CS orgs UP ACM and UP CURSOR, were given an opportunity to set up their own booths in order to introduce the diverse culture that they could offer through the Forbidden Forest or Organization Fair. The ESC also prepared fun team-building activities for the freshies and their blocs through the Search for the Horcruxes or Scavenger Hunt wherein a set of exciting missions were to be accomplished before the event’s culmination. In accordance with the Harry Potter theme, the Pensieve or Film Showing which was a Harry Potter movie marathon, was also held at the Engineering Theatre. On the final day of the event, the interdepartmental battle Quidditch Cup took place as well as the Shrieking Shack or Culminating Night wherein the winners were awarded. As the celebration came to an end, the OWeek fulfilled its aim to both promote unity among the different departments and the whole college, likewise to let the freshies showcase their competence and capabilities through a magical lineup of events. All photos courtesy of the Engineering Student Council.

title: Bootcamp 2.0: The 2015 CS Freshie’s Welcome
body: There may exist some freshies who are described as clueless, or 'nangangapa', fearing being hopelessly lost in a world of computer science. Others are fortunate: they already have some or a lot of knowledge on the subject. Maybe their first few months in college will be a breeze. But as their senpais, we all want them to feel comfortable, regardless of their background. That is why the DCSS and the CS Network organized Bootcamp 2.0. This week-long activity, held from 20-24 July, 2015, allowed the freshies to peek into the window of their BS CS life. While they were at it, they were also given the chance to mingle with each other and the upperclassmen. Freshies were divided into the four houses of Hogwarts, which served as their teams for the entire event. Three days were spent on lecture and coding, which they did in the morning. Afternoons were spent on games, talks, and activities organized by the CS Network, with CURSOR on day 2, CSI on day 3, and ACM on day 4. There was also a team-building activity and a cheering competition, as well as talks from professors and students. Bootcamp 2.0 served its purpose to acquaint the freshies with their CS lives, and we are glad to welcome them to the department with this event. We are hoping that they get to spearhead the next ones, too!

title: GeeCS 8.0: DCS Hackathon
body: The GeeCS 8.0: DCS Hackathon, an overnight competiton exclusive to the students of the Department of Computer Science, happened this October 17-18, with the theme being 'Good Governance and Public Service'. This event was organized by the UP Center for Student Innovations, and was sponsored by the Philippine Center for Investigative Journalism, who also provided data that the students could use for their applications. Out of the 12 teams that participated, Team V, with members Jerome Indefenzo, Gabrielle Torres, Kyle Rosales, and Karlo Colegio, won first place, taking home 10,000 pesos for their team. Following them were Teams PoTaPo,Zak and Nanay Patola, winning 7000 and 5000 pesos respectively. In addition to this, the event also gave away 5000 pesos to the two teams that created the best games, which were also Teams PoTaPo,Zak and Nanay Patola. Of course all teams were winners in their own way as they take home the experiences that they have learned during this hackathon, as well as tumblers courtesy of the organizers.

title: Algolympics 2016
body: Last February 20, 2016, UP Association for Computing Machinery held the Algolympics. title: DCS conducts Freshmen Assembly 2015
body: Roughly two months after the opening of classes, the Department of Computer Science held its Freshmen Assembly on the 28 th of September to orient the newcomers on the university rules that strongly needed to be disseminated. Headed by the faculty adviser Mr. Philip Christian Zuniga, the meet was attended by more than a hundred freshmen who paid keen attention to the retention rules for the department, for the College of Engineering, and for the university which were discussed. Moreover, Mr. Zuniga also took note of the scholastic or honor rules and other university processes that the freshmen might overlook. As new members of the CS community, it is also the freshmen’s responsibility to grasp the regulations that circulate around the department and to keep these in mind to avoid future conflicts and disagreements.

title: USC Diliman Holds SAIS Orientation
body: by Nina Quiazon The University Student Council (USC) Diliman held the first student-initiated orientation for the Student Academic Information System (SAIS) on August 18, 2015 at the Vidal A. Tan Hall. It was headed by USC Councilor Mark Navata, and was attended by representatives of different Student Councils, some faculty members, and students from UP Diliman. The orientation was divided into two parts: the presentation of eUP and SAIS, and the Q&A. The presentation was presided by Mr. Mico Chopitea and Ms. Sarah Joy Salvio, both members of the eUP Project. The Q&A was paneled by Dr. Jaime DL Caro, Project Director of the eUP Project, Dr. Anette Lagman, the eUP Project Consultant, and Dr. Eugene Rex Jalao, Business Analytics Program Director at the UP National Engineering Center. The eUP Project is the IT Infrastructure initiative of the UP President Alfredo E. Pascual’s Administration. Part of this initiative is the reinforcement of the University’s Information Systems, which includes Financial Management Information System (FMIS), Human Resources Information System (HRIS), Executive Information System (EIS), and Student Academic Information System (SAIS). You can hear the full audio recording embedded at this page, and read the full transcript of the orientation at bit.ly/SAISOrientation . All photos courtesy of Mr. Mark Navata.

title: First Homegrown PhD is DCS Chairman
body: Dr. Jan Michael Chi Yap is the new Chairman of DCS. Dr. Yap assumed the position last April 1, 2017. His ascendance to the position also marks the end of the term of previous chairman, Dr. Prospero Naval. Dr. Yap was appointed for a three-year term by COE Dean Rizalinda De Leon after a selection process was done. Dr. Jan Yap finished his BS, MS and PhD in Computer Science from the Department and he is the first product of the Department’s PhD program. Currently he is affiliated with the Algorithms and Complexity Lab. Before being appointed as chairman, he was Building Administrator and was also the previous leader of the DCS DRRMC. Concurrently, Dr. Yap is head of the Computational Genomics and Systems Biology Program of the Philippine Genome Center. Plans and Visions for the Department In his presentation to the Department faculty, Dr. Yap mentioned the importance of self assessment in the Department. The faculty body should be able to evaluate whether the Department is still going towards the right track or not. Another focus of his term is Extension Services. As UP Diliman DCS is the primary Computer Science unit in the Philippines, it should take a lead in working with Government and Industry with regards to ICT concerns. Dr. Yap also wants to institute permanence at the Department. He wants physical permanence, that can be attained when we have our own DCS building and operational permanence, as manifested by having a manual of operation or a catalog of Department processes. Dr. Yap also targets to have the Department’s proposal to be transformed as the Institute of Computing and Software Engineering be approved. Honoring Dr. Prospero Naval Dr. Pros Naval assumed the DCS chairmanship in April 2014. Upon his assumption as chairman, his term centered on three major issues: (1) The implementation of a Self-Assessment plan for DCS, (2)the formation of a new BSCS curriculum, in line of the changes that are brought upon by K-12 and of the 2013 ACM Curriculum Proposal, (3) construction of the DCS complex. Through his able leadership, Dr. Naval made great advances in all fronts. With the help of Jan Michael Yap, DCS was able to draft a document outlining the self assessment plan. Dr. Naval led in the development of a new BSCS proposal that was approved by the DCS faculty body and is already conditionally approved by the College’s executive board. Also, during the time of Dr. Naval, DCS was able to start construction and eventually finish off the first building (research wing) of the proposed DCS complex. Even after his term. Dr. Naval has committed to help in the managing the construction of the rest of the complex. First Actions Upon assumption as Chairman, Dr. Jan Yap hit the ground running by continuing the work on the BSCS proposal and securing funds for the DCS complex. Minimal reorganization was done. Dr. Wilson Tan retained his position as head of the Undergraduate Committee. Concurrently, Dr. Tan will serve as the first ever Assistant Chairman of DCS. Dr. Francis Cabarle will continue monitoring the construction of the DCS complex. Prof. Carlo Raquel will stay as the DCS building committee head. Ms. Riza Rae Pineda will continue as the DCS representative to the Faculty Socials Committee, and Mr. Edgar Felizmenio will continue as DCS representative to the College Information Committee. New appointments include Dr. Susan Festin who will be the new Graduate Committee head by next school year. Prof. Rommel Feria will be in-charge of Department Linkages. Article by: Philip Christian Zuniga

title: Class registration gets worse?
body: The registration for the first semester of the Academic Year 2015-2016 started this July 28, with the registration for us Computer Science students handled by both DCSS volunteers as well as our CS representatives. Numerous students flooded into the department during the three-day registration, including students from other degree programs who wished to enlist ES 26 classes. All in all, nothing particularly special to us CompSci students. Come the last day of enlistment however, and the experience became a bit unprecedented as an unusually large number of students were still underloaded. So much so, in fact, that the administration extended the deadline for enlistment to August 14. This situation was, according to the National Union of Students of the Philippines, because the UP administration 'carelessly took in more students, more than what it could actually accommodate, to compensate for the lack of enrollees next academic year due to the K to 12 program'. Thankfully some professors opened up and overbooked classes, solving the situation. This, however, has the unfortunate side effect of overworking them. But only time will tell if the UP administration will take concrete steps to solve this perpetual problem.

title: Tahniah! – Dinner for Graduating CS Students
body: Last July 18-19, 2016, the Computer Science Graduation Dinner was held at the Overlook Resort, Antipolo City. The event was organized by the Computer Science Representatives along with several Computer Science student volunteers. With its theme, 'Tahniah' (Malay word for 'Best Wishes' or 'Congratulations'), the graduation dinner aimed to be a memorable and wonderful event for the graduates of the Department of Computer Science. The event started with registration and a photo booth for the graduates with giveaways from PointWest and 100PLUS. A program was prepared for the graduates with Mark Daniel Asiddao, one of the CS Reps, giving his opening remarks. Ms. Maria May Mel Nicolas, a speaker from PointWest, also gave a short introduction about PointWest and its career opportunities. The awarding of outstanding students was also conducted by PointWest. The Service Award was given to Ms. Louvette Ting, who has been the most active DCS Server throughout the year, the Leadership Award to Ms. Elaine Iturralde, who has shown hard work and dedication for serving the Department and being a caring leader to the DCSS, and the Excellence Award to Mr. Jose Berba, who has attained the highest General Weighted Average in the graduating batch. Afterwards, the program then proceeded with the serving of dinner. After dinner, congratulatory speeches were also delivered by Ms. Aundrae Hernandez, the graduating batch’s adviser, and Mr. Mario Carreon, one of their professors during their entire CS life. Finally, Mr. Senen Albano, the batch representative, gave his inspiring words to the whole batch. Organization tributes from the UP Association for Computing Machinery (UP ACM), UP Association of Computer Science Majors (UP CURSOR), and UP Center for Student Innovations (UP CSI) were also presented. Finally, the program ended with the showing of the graduation tribute video prepared by the Graduation Dinner Team. After the program, the graduates spent the rest of the night bonding, swimming, and of course, no one would forget Sir Mario’s special tarot card reading booth. The Graduation Dinner was just one of the realizations for the graduates that their journey in UP has officially concluded. With the University Graduation held on June 26 and the College of Engineering Recognition Rites held on June 27, the graduates of the Department of Computer Science have come face to face with the reality that they have finally achieved what every UP student desires. The department is proud to present its high number of students who graduated with honors. SUMMA CUM LAUDE: MAGNA CUM LAUDE: CUM LAUDE:

title: Of Reductions, Adjustments and Proposals: the DCS General Education Consultation
body: The Department of Computer Science faculty held a General Education (GE) consultation/assembly for students last March 14, 2017 at the Accenture Hall. The GE Consultation was a briefing about the College of Engineering’s GE curricular proposal and the department’s proposal regarding the BS Computer Science (CS) curriculum. This was to inform the students of the pending revisions to the curriculum and to hear their opinion regarding the department’s proposal. According to Sir Philip Zuniga, who hosted the assembly, ' the students’ opinion is important to the faculty’s decision [on the curricular revision] '. Due to the implementation of the K-12 system, adjustments are to be made in the tertiary level. First, GEs included in the senior high school curriculum will either be repeated, modified or removed at college level. Second, adjustments to the number of GE units in the curriculum due to the deduction of one year to all five-year Engineering courses. With that, the College of Engineering has raised a proposal parallel to the current proposal by the university council; from the current hybridized GE program of forty-two units, wherein the student is required to enroll in 7 required GEs, to a scheme of fifteen plus six to twenty-four GE units, wherein the student would be required to enroll in only 5 required GEs. A collaboration between the College of Engineering with the College of Arts and Letters and/or College of Social Science and Philosophy was also proposed to develop courses which would be tied up with Engineering courses to ensure that the objectives for the General Education courses will remain intact. On the other hand, Ma’am Riza Rae Pineda raised her point against those who were against the reduction of the number of General Education courses to be taken. ' Dapat ma-offend kayo pag sinabi na mababawasan and nationalism niyo pag binawasan ang GE units, ' she stated, ' kasi parang sinasabi na pag nag-take kayo ng major subjects niyo, mababawasan ang sense of nationalism niyo. ', she added. In addition to the previous explanation about how and why both the University Council and the College of Engineering came up with their current proposals, Sir Zuniga also introduced the department’s proposed revisions to the BS CS curriculum, such as Should the proposed curriculum revision be implemented in the future, Sir Zuniga assured the students that the current batches (2013-2016) would not be affected by the proposal unless they are delayed by two academic years. Even though the current batches might not be affected or covered by the proposed curricular revision, he reminded the students that there is a tendency for some GEs to get phased out due to modifications and revisions in accordance to whichever proposal will be implemented.

title: CS innovation brought by dedication, service, and excellence
body: To excellently bring together the strengths of both engineering and science to advance society is truly a sight to behold. Such is the practice of UP Diliman’s Computer Science students, learning to make the most out of the traits and characteristics of being an engineer and a scientist. Innovation, creativity, ingenuity, these are but a few of many remarkable attributes that these students display in their effort to improve living conditions and to push the boundaries of technology. This academic year, there have been numerous competitions in the country that aim to address and bring about solutions to problems that plague our nation through computer science. Students from the Department of Computer Science joined these competitions, with many of them earning places and some even winning championships, using the things that they have learned to fully exhibit honor and excellence. Featured in this article are these teams and the competitions that they have attended. Photos from Cognizant PH and UP CSI Last November 26 – 27, 2016, a team composed of Joshua Frankie Rayo, John Robert Basiloña, Vince Lorbis, and Joseph Earl Branzuela from UP CSI bagged first prize for CogniCOP for CognizantPH HackNation: Decoding the Future . Hacknation is a programming and pitching competition that 'aims to enable students to create sustainable and accessible learning through hacked innovation.' It was held at the Science Hub Tower, McKinley Hill, Taguig. CogniCOP is a one-stop tool for Recruitment and Business Intelligence that aims to promote company transparency and recruitment integrity while developing recruiters’ passion through collaboration, and is driven towards customer care. Photo from devcon.ph On February 4, 2017, the finals for Devcon Devise were held at the PLDT TelTEC Innolab, Mandaluyong City. Devcon Devise is an application development competition that aims to challenge students in solving real world problems with mobile applications. Two teams from UP Diliman were a part of the said event. Team UP CSI was made up of John Robert Basiloña, Vince Lorbis, Joseph Earl Branzuela, and Sean Ryan Chan. The app they came up with was RefuGeo . It used the chikka messenger API integrated with features such as proper resource management, presenting ways on how to evacuate efficiently, and viewing of calamity trends through the use of Twitter. On the other hand, Team In-Code-Ables was composed of Eduardo Valdez, Christopher Vizcarra, Gabriel Kelly Navarro, and John Louise Tan. They presented the app Impulse . It focuses on aiding a victim with the help of an able-bodied person that may be present at the vicinity. Some features include the Pulse Checker, which is used to know the vital info of the victim and the Pulsifier, which is used to contact and inform the loved ones of the victim. UP DCS participants. Photo from HealthHacks PH That same weekend, February 4 and 5, 2017, HealthHacks PH was held at the Microsoft Office in Makati. HealthHacks PH is a healthcare hackathon that aims to promote healthcare entrepreneurship within the Philippines through innovative and efficient solutions. Five teams represented the Department of Computer Science for the said event. Three teams represented UP CSI, namely, Team Lino, Team Medicode, and Team T-Gang, and two teams, Team Paliparan and Happy Team, represented UP ACM. Team Paliparan, Team Lino, and Happy Team. Photos from HealthHacks PH Team Paliparan bagged the first prize for the event. Francis Adrian Galang, Jaye Marlice Hernandez, Clarence David Co, and Paola Faith Simon developed the app ProxiMed PH, which is a system software that optimizes and simplifies the transfer of patients among hospitals and other medical units using a simple interface that can easily send and receive transfer requests based on the availability of the hospitals. Team Lino bagged the third prize for the event. Lizzie Awa, Carl Araya, Luis Flores, and Trevor Uy presented HazMap , a website that gathers information on the frequency of illnesses within a city from hospitals and local health units, and uses the information to form an infographic map that showcases the most common diseases and the risk levels in the area. Happy Team, composed of Mary Abigail Castillo, Lizeth Baldelomar, and Frances Calceta, made it to the Top 6 teams. Their app, Blasst from the Passt , is an informative app that uses romance, specifically dating elements, to teach people about what parts of the human diet contributes to the texture and healthiness of someone’s excrement. Team Medicode. Photo from HealthHacks PH. Team Medicode, composed of Jose Carlos Rodrigo J. Azcarraga, Makki Villaluz, Carlo Jose Mediarito, and Sean Ryan Chan, presented their idea for an app, called MagnifEYE . It is an app that helps doctors in rural areas without modern equipment to be able to study a patient’s vital signs in real time using only his/her phone. Team T-Gang, composed of Christelle Divina, Jerrylee Isaac Geronimo, Jan Carlo Albano, and Gabriel Kelly Navarro, presented an app for creating medical contingency plans, such as assigning a healthcare proxy or someone you authorize to make medical and financial decisions on your behalf in case that you are unable to and drafting contracts or legal documents for that matter. On March 4-5, 2017, YouthHack 2017 was held at the Miriam College Henry Sy Sr. Innovation Center. YouthHack is an ideathon that walks students through the fascinating world of startups through workshops and talks. Two teams represented UP CSI for the event. Photo from Jose Carlos Rodrigo Azcarraga Maria April Rose Andaca, Lizzie Awa, Carl Araya and Luis Flores (HazMap) bagged the second place for their app Kantaught . It is a website wherein users can learn about sex education, take STD tests, and buy sex toys and contraceptives. The team Hackstreet Boys composed of Francis Zac Dela Cruz, Adel Mandanas, Ryan Supelana, and JM Pineda pitched their app, OneLiner . It’s a queueing system where anyone can start and end a queue. They are also planning to launch it before the semester ends. Photo from Engineering Innovations Congress On March 25, 2017, the Engineering Innovations Expo 2017 was held at Melchor Hall, UP Diliman. The exhibitXcompetition showcases Filipino innovations that drive local communities toward the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Team UP CSI composed of Joshua Frankie Rayo, John Robert Basiloña, Vince Lorbis, Joseph Earl Branzuela, and Sean Ryan Chan, bagged the first place with their app RefuGeo . More recently, last March 31, 2017 – April 1, 2017, Hackzilla in Manila 2, an intercollegiate hackathon with the theme of Artificial Intelligence, organized by Freelancer.com was conducted. Team UP CSI composed of John Robert Basiloña, Vince Lorbis, Joseph Earl Branzuela, and Sean Ryan Chan developed an app that uses data to stimulate human reason and intellect to answer 'What’s next?'. On the other hand, Team QTP2 composed of Jerrylee Isaac Geronimo, Gabriel Kelly Navarro, Jan Carlo Albano, and Zach Ubias made an app that aims to convert the user’s voice into a Filipino variant of the sign language used by deaf people and displays it on mobile phone’s screen, making it easier to communicate with them. While not all of these teams have won titles and places in the competitions, all of them are victors in their own right, as they have given everything that they have in their part to help people. While these victories do instill a sense of pride and accomplishment in the students, we must never forget that our goal, which to advance society through computer science, is far from being achieved, as there are still problems that afflict our nation, from petty troubles that slightly diminish living standards, to devastating issues that tear our society apart. There will be more competitions arriving in the coming months and years, serving as avenues for computer science students to do their part in helping to solve these problems. These competitions are great opportunities to be seized and difficult but fulfilling challenges to be conquered by the students of the University of the Philippines Diliman. Article by: Clarence David Co and Jaye Hernandez

title: CS Freshie Abaya, Newest Eng’g Freshie Rep to the UFC
body: by Christian Villamera Igniting the spark of service within the Engineering freshmen community, Raine Catherine Abaya represents the Department of Computer Science as she prevails as one of the three newly-elected Engineering Freshie Representatives to the University Freshie Council (UFC) with a total of 113 votes after the elections held on September 11 and 15, 2015. Through the R.A.I.N.E. mission (Reach, Accept, Involve, Nourish, and Engage), Abaya’s general plan of action is to draw out the potential of her fellow freshies’ inner willingness and desire to serve. She envisions a community with strong and authentic values, and an environment of trust and fairness where students are heard and listened to. Abaya will be working hand-in-hand with Anna Patricia Martinez and Katrina Maria Marcelo, both from the Department of Industrial Engineering, who garnered 148 votes and 118 votes respectively. With an aim to promote unity among freshmen and help them pursue academic excellence, camaraderie, and holistic growth, the three aspiring student leaders will strive to succeed in their endeavours throughout the academic year 2015-2016. Moreover, the two other contenders, John Moses Tan and Michael John Nacion, both also from IE Department, reaped 93 votes and 71 votes accordingly while 14 people abstained from voting. We wish them good luck, especially to our own Raine Catherine Abaya, and we hope that they will serve their constituents well.

title: UP Registration met Protests
body: The UP enrollment system met protests as the UP administration implemented the No Late Payment Policy on the first week of August. The usual extensive student lines stretched along corridors as students rushed to complete their units within a week. The No Late Payment Policy added burden to students who were financially in need. Their lack of units was further burdened by their need to immediately pay their tuition fee. Students resorted to camp-outs at the colleges just to ensure that they will get a subject. The acceptance of far greater students than normal intensified the high demand for subjects, already a yearly problem for the UP system.The petitions to accommodate the demand shifted the burden from the administration to the professors who had to open more classes than their expected work load and classroom capacity. The policy was reversed at the end of the week following numerous student activities.

title: #define Hackathon
body: Last February 27-28, seven teams from different universities competed in '#define Hackathon: Redefine the Future', a hackathon organized by the UP Epsilon Chi Fraternity. Competing overnight at Vinzons Hall, these teams had 24 hours to create a prototype that embodies the hackathon’s theme, 'Transportation, Energy, and the Environment'. Out of all these teams, two from the Department of Computer Science won. The champions were Team In-Code-Ables, composed of Christopher Ivan Vizcarra, Gian Carlo Sagun, John Louise Tan, Christiane Joy Angelyn Yee, and Eduardo Valdez, with their app 'KalyeSurvey'. It is a mobile application where people can use their time stuck in traffic in a more productive manner by being able to answer surveys on the go and earn points. Coming in at second place was Team No Sleep with members Frances Calceta, Rhea Limkinglam, and JM Manalo, with their visual novel titled 'Natural Boyfriend', where the player would be able to date a personification of nature while learning how to take care of the environment.

title: ESGS Philippines 2016
body: Last October 28-30, the eSports and Gaming Summit was held at the SMX Convention Center. This was one of the biggest events focused on the Philippine gaming scene, featuring various new games, gadgets, tournaments, and guests. Final Fantasy XV , Tekken 7 , Just Dance 4 , and NBA 2K17 were some of the games showcased in the three-day event. Companies such as Sony Playstation, Ubisoft, Capcom, MSI, PLDT, and Bandai-Namco were some of the major exhibitors. Tournaments for games like Dota 2 and League of Legends were also prepared. The Association for Computing Machinery – UP Diliman Student Chapter, Inc. (UP ACM) was also in the summit helping in the registration and showcasing games made by Computer Science students . Not new to this year’s ESGS was the Indie Arena, where games made by independent developers were showcased. One of the games exhibited was Javi Almirante’s, a 4th year UP Diliman Computer Science student, soon-to-be game, Experiments in Game Feel by Snack Studios. To know more about the game, feel free to watch this video . For more information about ESGS, visit the website here . Photos courtesy of Jaye Hernandez and Javi Almirante

title: Hackzilla In Manila
body: Last July 22-23, 12 teams participated in Hackzilla in Manila, an intercollegiate competition for young, talented Filipino designers, developers, and advocates organized by Freelancer.com, which aims to solve social issues with the use of technology. With its theme 'Preparing the Philippines to effectively respond to natural disasters through the use of technology', the college students developed at the Freelancer Office in Bonifacio Global City, Taguig. Among these teams, two out of the three teams from the University of the Philippines Diliman won the competition. The grand champions were Team In-Code-Ables, composed of Christopher Ivan Vizcarra, Eduardo Valdez, John Louise Tan, and Noli Valdez III, who won P50,000 with their app Impulse. In times of emergencies or calamities, their mobile app identifies the victim and notifies their loved ones in real-time. The app uses QR code technology to be able to scan the user’s significant data more securely. Coming in at second place were Ariz and the FEDs, composed of Joshua Ariz Charles Soriano, Gerard Ray Montemayor, and Aaron Diego Dimaano, who won P30,000 with their app Fortify. This can be used to prepare for potential disasters, and locate safe and hazardous zones to properly mobilize remote communities. For more information about the event itself, feel free to visit the Hackzilla Website . Photos courtesy of Diego Dimaano.

title: Podium Finish for UPD DCS Teams in Hack UP
body: by Abby Canedo and Patrick Dominguiano UP Diliman – Two teams from the Department of Computer Science, University of the Philippines Diliman secured a spot in the top 3 of Hack UP 2015 held at the GT-Toyota Asian Center and UP School of Economics Auditorium on September 26-27. Hack UP 2015 was the first intercollegiate 24-hour hackathon challenge hosted by the UP Center for Student Innovations (UP CSI) , UP Office of the Vice Chancellor for Research and Development (UP OVCRD) and 8Layer Technologies Inc. Over 30 teams from 13 different universities gathered to make software applications that revolved around this year’s theme which was 'Innovation for the Culture of the Nation'. Team In-code-ables composed of Gian Carlo Sagun, John Louise Tan, and Christopher Vizcarra from UP Diliman was hailed champion with their crowd-engagement app called 'Pulong' . It is a mobile application which aims to fix the Filipino-time mentality by enabling users to track the location or find out the estimated time of arrival of the attendees of an event. Meanwhile, Team Lifehacks with team members Zarah Arcega, Alvin Bravo, Anna Melgar, Jerome Tec and Wilbert Uy Jr. from Ateneo de Manila University bagged the 2 nd place with their winning web application called 'Tara' , a platform for organizing and joining culturally relevant events. Bringing home the 3 rd place was Team HackBBz composed of Jeynald Endaya, Agnes Jardaleza and Abbey Quiñones also from UP Diliman who created a data analytics tool called 'Identination' for regional trait analysis. It aims to gather data on the traits of people from the different regions of the Philippines and analyze these traits to find a common trait across all the regions in the country. The champion was able to take home 25,000 pesos, gold medals, a trophy for the team and another trophy for the school they are representing while the 2 nd and 3 rd place brought home 15,000 pesos with silver medals and 10,000 pesos with bronze medals respectively. Last January, four teams from UP CSI represented UP DCS in Ateneo Hacks (AHacks) where two of them also secured two spots in the top 3.
