title: Finding Success in Computer Science
body: DILIMAN, QC – With the assistance of the Computer Science Representatives (CS Reps), the Engineering Student Council (ESC) launched the 2015 Engineering Opening Week on August 29. However, instead of having the typical college orientation, the CS Reps invited various speakers to talk about finding success as a CS student. The purpose of the event was to inform students of the opportunities a degree in Computer Science can offer. Jeynald 'JJ' Endaya, president of UP CURSOR, delivered the introduction, emphasizing the importance of pursuing your passion and doing what’s meaningful to you. 'At the end of the day, life is just one big quest for meaning', he added. 'Why do you do what you do? The more you ask yourself this, the more you solidify your stand on why you took this course.' JJ interned at the multinational company Procter & Gamble last June. He is currently the software project leader of UP Bike Share, a bike-sharing system that will eventually use wireless technology for tracking and security . Startups Francis Uy, BS CS batch 1998, talked about startups, and shared the two principles that he lives by. 'First, live with integrity. Don’t leave people hanging and always remember, you are what you say. Second, start with a vision for yourself. Do what inspires you. Have a choice in what your future is going to be.' Mr. Uy has had 14 fruitful years of employment, from Accenture, to P&G, to his very own not one, but two companies, Sinag Solutions and Mobkard. When asked about his parting message to the audience, he replied,'Things are gonna be okay. Enjoy the ride. Life is going to turn out fine'. UP Student Community Catherine Angangco, founder of UP CSI, discussed org life. She highly encouraged students to join organizations that are in line with their hobby, passion, or interest. 'Be part of something. If you don’t, you’ll only experience half of what UP has to offer'. Cat is currently working as a systems analyst at P&G. Freelancing Arian Allenson Valdez, head of CSI’s Development Committee, expounded on freelancing, describing the pleasures of making your own time and being your own boss. In addition to this, he also stressed the importance of finding your niche, a specialized but profitable segment of the market. Lastly, he shared that the keys to success in freelancing are fast iteration times, solid track record, and spending time understanding the business. Among other things, Arian developed the Rate U.P. Professors website, a professor review curation website that UP students access. And the most amazing part? He’s only a sophomore. —————————– Equipped with the wisdom imparted by the speakers, the students are geared to take on the CS life and combat the challenges that lie ahead. Thus they begin their quest for success in CS!

title: The UP Parser, Best Department Newsletter 2016-2017
body: Last April 20, 2017, the Engineering Student Council held its Turnover Ceremony at the Maynilad Room, College of Engineering. Both members of the outgoing ESC 1617 and the incoming ESC 1718 were present as the beginning of a new term is about to come. Outstanding members of the outgoing ESC were given various awards based on their performance in the council. One of these exceptional awards, 'The Best Department Newsletter', was awarded to the Department of Computer Science’s very own, The UP Parser. The UP Parser was also awarded as the best department newsletter during the ESC 15-16 Turnover Ceremony. This makes Parser’s 2 nd consecutive year of the award. Since its revival in 2015, Parser continues to bring excellence and service through department and collegiate journalism. With the recent addition of an editorial board this school year, Parser has expanded its horizons and goals. Parser has also actively participated in department events documentation. Live coverage through videos, tweets, and pictures has increased information dissemination. Lastly, a distinguishing feat that Parser has achieved this year is its first event, PicSel, which was held during GeeCS 9.0. Given the accomplishments of Parser, the work and dedication does not stop there as Parser will continue to actively become an avenue of more awareness and involvement in the Department of Computer Science.

title: GalactiCS: CS Week 2016
body: The Computer Science Week 2016 was held last April 25-29 with the theme GalactiCS. This year’s CS Week aimed to bring the department together and to help people appreciate what it feels like to be Computer Science students. April 25 marked the start of the Great Space Race which was organized by the UP Association for Computing Machinery and which lasted throughout the entire week. People dropped by the CS Week Exhibit brought to you by the UP Center for Student Innovations. They also got the chance to vote for the Pop Icon 2016 by dropping their cash votes into the Pop Icon box. Winners of the contest were announced in the Computer Science Week Facebook page. Also, people took the opportunity to appreciate the faculty, the admin, and the staff of the department by sending messages through the Thanksgiving Box. CS Week had its Opening Day last April 26, followed by the different events of the Batch Competition, which includes Pasta Relay on the 26th, Fly Me to the Moon on the 27th, Alien Colors on the 28th, and finally, Flip Games on the 29th. This Batch Competition, held by the UP Association of Computer Science Majors, had Draco as this year’s champion, followed by Cygnus, Aquila, and Orion respectively. To celebrate and end this week-long event, CS Students bonded in Space Bar, the CS Week Party. With free-flowing drinks, neon lights, and a wide range of party activities, Space Bar took everyone that participated to a whole new world last April 29 at the Torre Venezia Suites. For more information, you can visit their official Facebook page, 'Computer Science Week'.

title: Bootcamp 4.0: Freshies are Coming
body: Last July 25-27, 2017, Bootcamp 4.0 was held at the UP Department of Computer Science to welcome the new students of the department. This event is traditionally conducted by the department’s volunteer corps, the DCS Servers, in partnership with The UP Parser, UP Association for Computing Machinery (UP ACM), UP Center for Student Innovations (UP CSI), and UP Association of Computer Science Majors (UP CURSOR). It aims to gather all freshies, shiftees and transferees to be officially welcomed to the department, which will be their second home. With this year’s theme, Game of Thrones, they were divided into three houses – Lannister, Stark, and Targaryen – and the organizations prepared Game of Thrones-themed activities for the participants. This 3-day event is composed of different activities – where morning session is reserved for lecture and coding workshops, while afternoon sessions is for organizations’ talks and games. In this way, the students are not only learning, but also having a fun time to bond with their batchmates whom they will be with through their stay here in UP. On its first day, opening remarks were given by the Computer Science Representatives (CS Reps). Mr. Roy Canseco delivered the inspirational message. After those talks was an introductory talk of Almond Valleza about the UP Parser’s programs and agenda being the Department’s Official Publication. UP ACM then hosted the afternoon sessions. The following days were composed of morning and afternoon sessions where UP CSI and UP CURSOR hosted their activities respectively. On the last day of the event, House Lannister conquered the Iron Throne. This series of events may come to an end, but for them it is just the beginning – beginning of their life being a college student, an Isko/Iska, of the Department Computer Science. Be prepared to 'hear them roar'! Article by: Rogiella Isidro

title: DCS welcomes new CS students in Bootcamp 3.0
body: The Department of Computer Science (DCS) officially welcomed the incoming Computer Science (CS) freshmen, shiftees, and transferees altogether for the first time in Bootcamp 3.0 held at the Alumni Engineers Centennial Hall on July 26-28. Bootcamp 3.0 was a 3-day pokemon-themed event organized by the CS Representatives and the CS Network which featured basic and intermediate crash courses in C programming, various fun activities prepared by the CS organizations, and a series of talks about the university, department, and the Computer Science program. Unlike the previous bootcamps which only accommodated incoming freshmen students, the CS Representatives and Overall Event Co-Heads decided to make Bootcamp 3.0 as the official welcoming event for all new CS students, which included not only the freshies but also the shiftee and transferee students as well. 'We decided to make Bootcamp 3.0 as the official welcoming event for all new CS students in order to congratulate them for making it into the Computer Science program and to immerse them at the same time to the environment of their new home by letting them meet some of the faculty members, upperclassmen, and batchmates,' Marc Almond Valleza, one of the CS Representatives, said. Valleza added that Bootcamp 3.0 also served as the perfect avenue for the new CS students to get to know more about the CS curriculum, department and university policies, and different CS organizations. Aside from getting to know more about the university and the department, the basic and intermediate crash courses in C programming, handled by Ma’am Marie Jo-Anne Mendoza and Sir Juan Felipe Coronel respectively, were the core of the 3-day event. These crash courses covered different topics in basic programming to give the new students a brief overview on the lessons that they will encounter in their first programming class. 'The classes were genuinely fun and I really learned a lot! It also motivated me to practice C at home to prepare for the start of classes,' Justine Paul Alvaro, a shiftee from the BS Statistics program, stated. 'It also gave me a newfound love in coding,' he added. On the other hand, the different CS organizations namely UP Association of Computer Science Majors (UP CURSOR), UP Association for Computing Machinery (UP ACM), and UP Center for Student Innovations (UP CSI), added fun and excitement in the bootcamp through the various activities that they had prepared. In general, all activities of the organizations focused on giving the new students a creative walkthrough on what they would expect to learn and encounter over the years of their stay in the curriculum. According to Anica Galano, a freshman student, meeting the different organizations and people from the department was the best part of the bootcamp because she was able to get a feel of the department and it solidified her desire to go to CS. The 3-day event concluded through an inspirational closing speech given by Sir Paul Rossener Regonia followed by a simple pakain for all the participants and event organizers.

title: REV34L: CURSOR Week 2017
body: On its 34th year, UP Association of Computer Science Majors (CURSOR) conducted a week-long celebration composed of different events and activities prepared not only for its members, but also for the whole Department of Computer Science studentry. This year’s theme – 'REV34L', showcases their organization’s established identity in the university. Starting the event, last August 15, CURSOR opened an exhibit where it advertised its image by featuring different highlights of the organization such as its new logo and brand book. This has been done to raise awareness, not only for the members, but also for everyone else to know what UP CURSOR is all about. In conjunction to its opening day, the Committee Wars was also announced internally. This was made a week-long event as it is composed of numerous sport and team building activities for the committees inside the organization. This aims to promote the spirit of kinship, sportsmanship and competence among its members. On the night of August 16, a picnic is set to bring CURSOR members together to hang out and chill while watching movies. Being a socio-academic organization, social wellness is monitored throughout its members. This event is made to form stronger bonds and foster camaraderie inside the organization. The following day, August 17, the Academic Event of this year named 'Chalk Talk' was held at CLR 4, UP AECH. The concept and applications of Internet of Things (IoT) was tackled by speakers namely, Nestor Michael Tiglao and Alexander Calero. CURSOR aims to feed Computer Science students with new ideas and knowledge that is not taught inside a classroom. But this year’s Academic Event isn’t just a normal event every year because there has been an unexpected revelation for its members. In line with its theme – 'REV34L', one of the speakers, Alexander Calero, ended his talk with a revelation on what’s the real origin of the name CURSOR. Being a CS alumnus of batch ‘80, he witnessed the creation of CURSOR thus the real reason why CURSOR is its name. After all these years, members of the organization were finally enlightened with CURSOR’s history. To culminate the week-long event, on August 18, CURSOR Night was held on the Don Victorino Clubhouse, Quezon City. This CURSOR Night was different from the previous CURSOR Nights as this was a formal ball. CURSOR members and other guests wore their best suits and gowns. The program consisted of performances from the CURSOR Jammeng’g band, 'Plain Seas', and the Engineering Week Awitan 1st runner-up, the CURSOR Choir. Video messages from CURSOR alumni were also presented to everyone. After the program proper, everyone spent the rest of the night away with music, dancing, foods, and drinks. Every year, this organization continues to grow along with the other organizations of the Computer Science Network. What will CURSOR bring to us this academic year? Article by: Rogiella Isidro and Almond Valleza Photos from: CURSOR Week

title: The Pulse Is Back
body: The Impulse, the official publication of the UP ESC (Engineering Student Council), returned from its 3-year hiatus as part of the UP ESC’s vision of Unparalleled Service. It aims to continue its legacy of informing engineering students about pressing issues, latest news, and updates about the College of Engineering. Its comeback issue was published last November 19, 2016 on their Facebook page containing news about the College of Engineering’s new dean and the University Student Council’s new chairperson, as well as articles about the college’s Engineering Week. The publication’s Editorial Board, lead by John Robert Basiloña as its Editor-in-Chief, with the help of ESC’s Project Coordinators Ivan Geronimo and Mac Pasciolco, and ESC’s Public Relations Councilor Karla Ebol, aim to be guided by transparency, accountability, and integrity in their articles. The first issue of Impulse can be viewed at http://bit.ly/IMPULSEv3i1 .

title: Bootcamp 2.0
body: Episode 1 of 10(1516A) Made with a twist of 8-bit Harry Potter, which is the theme of Bootcamp 2.0. This episode recaps the events from the very first Bootcamp of DCS (AY1415) and announces a SECOND Bootcamp on the 20th to the 24th of July, year 2015. Watch out freshmen!

title: Finals Week
body: Episode 7 of 10 (1516A) It’s just going to be a few more weeks before the semester finally ends. So we’ve asked DCS students for tips and advice regarding finals week and how you can survive it.

title: DCS Groundbreaking
body: The groundbreaking for the department’s new building held last April 21st marked the beginning of the department’s new chapter. Named the Innovation Wing, it is the first of three buildings that the Department of Computer Science will be having. This new building will be located near the Office of the University Registrar and behind the new School of Statistics building. According to Chancellor Michael Tan, this particular area of the Engineering Complex will be named the Computational Sciences Hub. Aside from the Statistics building, the Department of Computer Science will also have the Department of Industrial Engineering and Operations Research as their neighbors. He added that Melchor Hall will be given to the College of Arts and Sciences once the entire College of Engineering has migrated to the complex. President Alfredo Pascual mentioned the planned renaming of the department to the 'Institute of Computer Science and Software Engineering'. He also made mention that 3.5 billion Pesos was allocated to finish the Engineering Complex. Among the other speakers was Dean Aura Matias, the College of Engineering dean, who gave the opening remarks. She also explained the purpose of the two other buildings of the department, one for academics and one for the faculty.

title: UP CURSOR ranks 7th in Engineering Week 2017
body: This year, the Engineering Week (Eng’g Week), an annual event by the College of Engineering and hosted by the Engineering Student Council (ESC), was held last February 22 until March 4, 2017. 16 organizations from the college participated in the week-long event, including the UP Association of Computer Science Majors (UP CURSOR), the only organization in the UP Computer Science Network that participates in the event. On the first day, February 22, UP CURSOR participated in 'Siklabo: Smoker Night 2017', which marked the beginning of the night events of the Eng’g Week. This event’s primary purpose was to exercise the creativity of the Engineering students to discuss relevant social issues in a comedic way. The humorous atmosphere of event helped boost the excitement and spirit of the engineering students. UP CURSOR, as a participating organization, joined almost all of the day events of Eng’g Week, one of them being 'Kabayeng’g' where they were crowned as back-to-back champions. In addition to this, they also ranked first in 'Songwriteng’g 2017'. Subsequently, the organization also showcased their holistic talents in the night events of Eng’g Week. UP CURSOR’s Awitaners came in second place in Awitan 2017, where they performed the songs Vuelie and Bui Doi . They also joined MSK 2017, with their MASKI Group as the 1st runner-up and their MASKI Pair as the 2nd runner-up. Other awards that UP CURSOR achieved came from Filmfest 2017. The awards won under Category A were as follows: Je Marie Apolinario as Best Actress, Neal Bryan Velasco as Best Actor, Edrich Chua as Best Director, Best Screen Play, and 2nd Best Picture. As for Category B, UP CURSOR won the Best Picture award. To keep track of the scores, the leaderboard was posted by the ESC in the Engineering Week Facebook page. UP CURSOR got into the top 10 organizations at the 6th day of Eng’g week, February 27. Starting from 10th place, they rose to the top 8 the following day. After the event, overall rankings were posted by the ESC, with UP CURSOR finishing at 7th place, a rank higher than the previous year’s Eng’g Week final standings.

title: Eng’g Conversations x Balitaktakan: An Educational Discussion on eUP
body: On October 6, 2015, the University Student Council’s Balitaktakan and the Engineering Student Council’s Engineering Conversations co-presented a forum regarding eUP at the Engineering theater. This event talked about the current status of eUP, shedding light on the questions that people had regarding this project. Among the invited speakers, Dr. Ramon Guillermo talked about the advantages of open-source software, while at the same time hitting the administration for not doing so with regards to the eUP project. He also questioned the budget of the project, requesting an audit of all eUP expenses, as well as the long-term effects of the project. Lastly, he demanded from the administration an independent audit of all eUP expenses, including the audit of the auction for the system. President Pascual, on the other hand, countered the points of the first speaker with regards to the budget of the eUP project. He explained that, in fact, the actual Oracle system costs only 215.1 million pesos, only a small portion of the 745.2 million allocated to the entire project. He also explained that whether or not the Oracle system is implemented, the administration will still spend the remaining 500 million pesos to improve the ICT of UP, like increasing the system bandwidth available to UP by almost 5Gbps. In addition to this, President Pascual promised to publish the budget related to the eUP project to the UP website. Furthermore, he explained that the administration did not get the budget for this project from other UP services and that, in fact, the well-known 2.2 billion budget cut of UP was from the capital outlays of the Philippine General Hospital, and not from the academic units of UP. To show proof, he also presented the outline of UP’s budget, remarking that the total budget allocated for the academic units would even increase from 2015 to 2016. What followed next was a Q&A session, with the audience being able to ask questions directly to President Pascual one by one. The questions primarily focused on the budget, with other questions shedding more light on the topics not covered by the presentation, like the eventual fate of CRS and the benefits that the new system would have over the current system. Lastly, four student reactors went up to the stage to comment on the event. After the event, numerous netizens commented on the forum, with many criticizing the supposedly amateurish user interface of the system. Some people also bemoaned the lack of technical details of the project, commenting on the lack of transparency on this aspect of the project as way for the administration to hide from criticism. Meanwhile, others felt insulted that the administration does not wish to develop the system in-house, feeling that this makes a mockery of their skills.

title: GeeCS 8.0: DCS’s Magical Week
body: For the students at DCS, the third week of October was not just a week to do MPs or study for exams. We celebrated our annual GeeCS, now on it’s eighth year, with a Disney theme so everyone can have a feel of the magic that computer science brings. The weekend before the event opened, UP Center for Student Innovations held its annual department hackathon, a way for students to apply what they have learned thus far and find solutions to real-life problems. The hackathon was held at the Lecture Hall from 17-18 October, with the theme governance and public service for the participants to revolve their innovations around. GeeCS 8.0 officially started on Tuesday, 20th October. Students gathered at the DCS lobby to hear about what the week had in store, with the head of each event giving their introduction. After which, free ice cream was served! The second day of GeeCS 8.0 focused on the social awareness of the students. For this year’s Social Awareness Day, which was held at the ERDT room, the talk was about Game Authorship. The speaker for this event was Mr. Nigel Rodriguez, a homegrown of the Department. In the afternoon, the semestral State of the Department Assembly (SODA) was held. The SODA is an avenue for the department to discuss the existing matters that press concerns. Additionally, the Engineering Student Council and the University Student Council were present and were also able to discuss other matters. Day three was a day specifically given to the organizations of the department. The UP Association for Computing Machinery set up a game room at the Seminar Room, where students went to take a quick break from the day and played to their heart’s content. In the evening, the UP Association of Computer Science Majors held CS#. This event discussed the importance of building startups, as UP CURSOR promotes this culture as well. GeeCS 8.0 capped off with the quiz show Beat the GeeCS. Fortunately, this quiz show did not test the knowledge of the students (not just in the department) about computer science, but rather what they know about Disney movies! Teams bested each other with their Disney-powered minds and hearts. The entire event was successful in showcasing every aspect of a CS student: their knowledge in the field, their awareness with their surrounding environment, and the joy of being in the department. GeeCS 8.0 unleashed the magic that we possess, and the child inside us all.

title: GeeCS 9.0 : Build, Discover, Develop
body: Last November 7–11, 2016, GeeCS 9.0 was held by the members of the UP Computer Science Network, composed of the Department of Computer Science Servers (DCSS), UP Association for Computer Machinery (UP ACM), UP Center for Student Innovations (UP CSI), UP Association of Computer Science Majors (UP CURSOR), and The UP Parser. Celebrating its 9th year, GeeCS 9.0 emphasized that students are more than just students – that they have the capacity to go beyond their academics despite catering still to their basic human necessities. Unlike the tradition of GeeCS, this year’s GeeCS’ events focused on promoting the student organizations. On its first day, November 7, The UP Parser conducted an event called PiCSel: Introduction to Basic Photography. Mr. Jasper Mallonga, a member of the UP Photography Society (UP OPTICS), introduced simple terminologies and demonstrated different techniques in photography. The audience participated by trying out how ISO, shutter speed, and aperture works. The following day, November 8, the UP CURSOR exhibited different activities primarily focused on students having a break from their academically loaded lives. The UP CURSOR’s Activity Stations & Fair presented different fun and exciting activities from arts, music, board games, to chill stations. Next, November 9, is the Game Room by the UP ACM. Students came inside the room mainly to hangout. They also featured games like Super Smash Bros, Mario Kart, Just Dance, etc. On November 10, the UP CSI organized a pre-hackathon training for the participants of the upcoming hackathon with Mr. Topher Vizcarra, Mr. Louise Tan, and Mr. Alexis Collado as the speakers. They mentored about basic UI/UX and Git to prepare them for the actual hackathon the following day. Finally, a department-wide hackathon was held on November 11–12, with 8 teams participating, namely ADBAESOR, ChillHax, Power Rangers, Team Paliparan, JERRILY, DCS Dev Squad, P.io, and Hydro. Bagging first place was Power Rangers with Diliman Source: a registration platform for independent developers. Chillhax, and P.io respectively won second and third place by a very small margin. With this, GeeCS 9.0 showed the Computer Science student body that students are more than just students. From organizing and executing the events, to competing in different aspects, students can actually do a lot more beyond their potentials.

title: The UP Parser Receives Best Student Publication Award
body: The UP Parser was awarded twice during the Engineering Student Council (ESC) turnover ceremony held last May 3 at the National Engineering Center Auditorium. The official student publication of the Department of Computer Science was distinguished as The Best Department Publication by the ESC. The council also recognized UP Parser’s CS Flash as the recipient of the #ServiceRedefined Citation for Creativity. Photos courtesy of Elaine Iturralde The ceremony was attended by both outgoing and incoming members of the council, as well as the Dean of the College of Engineering, Dr. Aura Matias, and the Associate Dean for Student Affairs, Dr. Joanna Zomil Resurreccion. After the oath-taking of the newly elected officers, the former student leaders presented their achievements and projects during their term. Other CS related awardees include GeeCS 8.0, which won the Best Department/Institute Event, CS Week’s Space bar: CS Party, which won the Best Culmination Event, and former CS Representative Elaine Iturralde, who was named as one of the Most Outstanding Department/Institute Representative.

title: Bootcamp 5.0: Freshies, Assemble!
body: Bootcamp 5.0 was held at the UP Department of Computer Science last July 26-28, 2018. The three-day event aims to welcome the incoming freshies and introduce them to life in the department. The Computer Science Representative, Pio Fortuno and the department’s volunteers, the DCS Servers, along with the department’s organizations, UP Association for Computing Machinery (UP ACM), UP Center for Student Innovations (UP CSI), and UP Association of Computer Science Majors (UP CURSOR), the department’s student publication, The UP Parser, all worked together to make this event possible. On its fifth year, Bootcamp still holds the usual lectures and coding workshops to give them the basics im their first classes. The organizations are also given an opportunity to introduce themselves by preparing activities that aim to introduce the freshies to other aspects of life in the department. In line with this year’s theme, Avengers: Infinity War, the participants were divided into five teams: the Avengers, the Asgardians, Team Wakanda, the Guardians of the Galaxy, and the Agents of S.H.I.E.L.D. These were their teams in participating in the organizations’ events in hopes of encouraging the batch to bond and make friends even before class starts. On the first day, Pio opened the event along with Sir Mario Carreon, who gave an inspiring message to welcome the freshies. After the talks, The UP Parser held their first ever mini-workshop series for the event, introducing the freshies to the publication. The UP Parser also participated in the Bootcamp competition by giving tasks based on the work it does. In the afternoon, UP ACM took over for the first organization event, where they held their own Avengers-themed amazing race. The second day’s event, BootUp 5.0: Avenges Assembly, was held by UP CSI. The teams gathered the Avengers through the stations and tasks and had a scavenger hunt in search for the Infinity Stones. On the third day, UP CURSOR closed the organization events with their own twist on the theme, Race to Build the Infinity Gauntlet, based on a popular theory for the next movie in the series. The teams gathered parts of the Infinity Gauntlet throughout the race and were able to complete it in the end. After the last event, the winning team was awarded. Congratulations to the Asgardians for winning Bootcamp 5.0, and congratulations to everyone else for participating. All the teams made such wonderful outputs for our tasks and we appreciate everyone’s effort (You may visit the teams’ Facebook pages through these links: Asgardians , Avengers , Guardians , S.H.I.E.L.D. , and Wakanda ). This event may have come to an end but it’s just the beginning for the freshies. Good luck on your first year, freshies. Batch 2018, assemble! Article by: Carlo La Rosa

title: CURSOR Week
body: Episode 2 of 10 (1516A): In this episode of CS Flash, we report about CURSOR’s 32nd anniversary celebration, covering its activities and events planned for the next two weeks. Starring Ma’am Rae Pineda, an alumna of UP CURSOR.

title: UP CURSOR Launches its Newest Logo
body: The UP Association of Computer Science Majors (CURSOR) introduced its new logo to the public as part of the organization’s rebranding on February 12 of this year, which aims to give emphasis to the main goal of the organization – holistic development. ' The mission and vision is not well represented by the old logo. In fact, the meaning of the old logo is lost from the members. Kaya ayan, we made a new logo that would represent what we want to achieve for the members. ', said Neal Bryan Velasco, head of the rebranding team. Representing the organization’s seven committees are the seven rays at the lower-left part of the logo. Each of the four nodes that makes up the cursor in the center represents four of the five core values of UP CURSOR, which are camaraderie, selfishness, respect, and resourcefulness. Lastly, the hexagon which encloses the different features of the logo represents the last core value and the main goal of the organization, which is holistic development. People unaffiliated with the organization generally gave a positive response towards the rebranding and the new logo.

title: A Hui Hou Kakou
body: A Hawaiian-themed Graduation Dinner awaited the seniors of the Department of Computer Science last June 6, 2015 at the Bretsch and Barrie Resort, where they were given leis (flower wreaths presented in Hawaii as a symbol of affection to guests upon arrival or departure) after registration. After most of them arrived, the program started with a prayer followed by dinner service. Ms. John Andreau Hernandez, Romeo Senen Albano, and Maria April Rose Andaca, with their contagious energy, brought life to the event as the hosts of the night. Furthermore, Sir Mario Carreon delivered his yearly-anticipated epic speech while Sir Paul Regonia gave a relatable speech on the undergraduate life and the future ahead of them after graduation. Chosen representatives from each thesis laboratory were also given the chance to share their experiences and innermost thoughts for their fellow batchmates. Audio-visual presentations dedicated to the graduates’ stay in the department were presented on screen, as well as videos from the CS network paying tribute to the graduating members of their organization. To end the program, the seniors participated in games such as Pepero Kiss and Card Kiss. But the night was still young and definitely not coming to an end soon as they had the pool, free flowing drinks, karaoke machine, barbeque, and each other’s company all throughout the night to enjoy until they meet again—A Hui Hou Kakou.
